questions.stem: The fundamental group of the circle $S^1$ is:
questions.explanation: $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$, generated by the loop that goes once around. The isomorphism sends a loop to its winding number (degree). This is the foundational computation in algebraic topology.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The generator of $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$ corresponds to a loop with winding number:
questions.explanation: The standard generator is the loop $\omega(s) = (\cos 2\pi s, \sin 2\pi s)$, which winds once around the circle, so its winding number (degree) is $1$. Its powers $\omega^n$ have winding number $n$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The isomorphism $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$ is established using:
options: the covering space $\mathbb{R} \to S^1$ and the lifting properties of paths and homotopies.
questions.explanation: The exponential covering $p : \mathbb{R} \to S^1$, $p(t) = (\cos 2\pi t, \sin 2\pi t)$, has unique path and homotopy lifting. The endpoint of the lift of a loop, an integer, gives the isomorphism with $\mathbb{Z}$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The fundamental group of $\mathbb{R}^n$ is:
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{R}^n$ is convex, hence contractible, so $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^n)$ is trivial. Every loop contracts to its basepoint via the straight-line homotopy.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Any convex subset $C$ of $\mathbb{R}^n$ has fundamental group:
questions.explanation: A convex set is contractible: the straight-line homotopy $H(x,t) = (1-t)x + t x_0$ stays inside $C$. Hence $\pi_1(C)$ is trivial, regardless of shape or size.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The fundamental group of the torus $T^2 = S^1 \times S^1$ is:
options: the free group on two generators.
questions.explanation: Since $\pi_1$ of a product is the product of the $\pi_1$'s, $\pi_1(T^2) \cong \pi_1(S^1) \times \pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$. The two generators are the meridian and longitude loops, which commute.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the fundamental group of the torus abelian?
options: No, it is the nonabelian free group $F_2$.
Only after passing to a subgroup.
questions.explanation: $\pi_1(T^2) \cong \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$ is abelian. Geometrically, the two circle factors can be slid past each other, so the meridian and longitude loops commute up to homotopy.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The fundamental group of the n-torus $T^n = (S^1)^n$ is:
options: $\mathbb{Z}^n$, the free abelian group of rank $n$.
questions.explanation: By the product formula iterated, $\pi_1(T^n) \cong \mathbb{Z}^n$, free abelian of rank $n$. The $n$ circle factors give $n$ commuting generators.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The fundamental group of a wedge of two circles (the figure eight) is:
options: the free group $F_2$ on two generators.
questions.explanation: By the Seifert-van Kampen theorem, $\pi_1(S^1 \vee S^1)$ is the free product $\mathbb{Z} * \mathbb{Z}$, the free group $F_2$ on two generators. It is nonabelian.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The fundamental group of a wedge of $n$ circles is:
questions.explanation: A wedge of $n$ circles has fundamental group the free group $F_n$ on $n$ generators, one loop per circle. For $n \ge 2$ it is nonabelian.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The sphere $S^2$ is:
questions.explanation: $S^2$ is simply connected: it is path-connected and $\pi_1(S^2)$ is trivial. Any loop on the sphere can be contracted to a point, for example by pushing it off a missed point and using stereographic projection.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: For $n \ge 2$, the fundamental group of the sphere $S^n$ is:
questions.explanation: For $n \ge 2$, $S^n$ is simply connected, so $\pi_1(S^n)$ is trivial. A loop misses some point and so lies in $S^n$ minus a point, which is homeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^n$ and contractible.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The fundamental group of the real projective plane $\mathbb{RP}^2$ is:
questions.explanation: $\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^2) \cong \mathbb{Z}/2$. The double cover $S^2 \to \mathbb{RP}^2$ has $S^2$ simply connected, so $\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^2)$ is the deck group, of order $2$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: For $n \ge 2$, the fundamental group of real projective space $\mathbb{RP}^n$ is:
questions.explanation: For $n \ge 2$, $\mathbb{RP}^n = S^n / (x \sim -x)$ has simply connected double cover $S^n$, so $\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^n) \cong \mathbb{Z}/2$, the group of deck transformations.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In $\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^2) \cong \mathbb{Z}/2$, the generator is a loop that lifts to a path in $S^2$ joining:
options: no two points; it does not lift.
the north and south poles only.
questions.explanation: The nontrivial element of $\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^2)$ lifts to a path in $S^2$ from a point $x$ to its antipode $-x$. Going around twice lifts to a path from $x$ back to $x$, which is null-homotopic, reflecting the relation that the generator squares to the identity.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The Mobius band has fundamental group:
questions.explanation: The Mobius band deformation retracts onto its core circle, so $\pi_1$ of the Mobius band is $\mathbb{Z}$, the same as for the circle.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The cylinder $S^1 \times [0,1]$ has fundamental group:
questions.explanation: The cylinder deformation retracts onto its core circle $S^1 \times \{0\}$, so $\pi_1(S^1 \times [0,1]) \cong \pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$. Equivalently $[0,1]$ is contractible, so it contributes a trivial factor.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The punctured plane $\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{0\}$ has fundamental group:
questions.explanation: The punctured plane deformation retracts onto the unit circle, so $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{0\}) \cong \mathbb{Z}$, detected by winding number around the removed point.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which of these spaces has trivial fundamental group?
questions.explanation: $S^2$ is simply connected, so $\pi_1(S^2)$ is trivial. The circle, torus, and figure eight all have nontrivial fundamental groups ($\mathbb{Z}$, $\mathbb{Z}^2$, and $F_2$ respectively).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Because $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$ is nontrivial while $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2)$ is trivial, we conclude:
options: $S^1$ is not a retract of $\mathbb{R}^2$ and the two are not homotopy equivalent.
questions.explanation: Different fundamental groups show $S^1$ and $\mathbb{R}^2$ are not homotopy equivalent. The same fact shows $S^1$ is not a retract of the disk $D^2$, the key step in proving the Brouwer fixed point theorem.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The fundamental group of the Klein bottle is:
options: the nonabelian group $\langle a, b \mid abab^{-1} \rangle$.
questions.explanation: The Klein bottle has fundamental group $\langle a, b \mid abab^{-1} = 1 \rangle$, equivalently $\langle a, b \mid bab^{-1} = a^{-1} \rangle$, which is nonabelian. Its abelianization is $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}/2$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Using $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$, a continuous map $f : S^1 \to S^1$ induces $f_* : \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$ which is multiplication by:
questions.explanation: A self-map of the circle induces multiplication by its degree on $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$. For example $z \mapsto z^n$ has degree $n$ and induces multiplication by $n$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The genus-two orientable surface (double torus) has fundamental group that is:
options: nonabelian, with four generators and a single relation.
questions.explanation: The closed orientable surface of genus $g$ has fundamental group $\langle a_1, b_1, \ldots, a_g, b_g \mid \prod_i [a_i, b_i] = 1 \rangle$. For $g = 2$ there are four generators and one commutator-product relation; the group is nonabelian.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which statement correctly compares the torus and the wedge of two circles?
options: The torus has abelian $\pi_1$ while the figure eight has nonabelian $\pi_1$, so they are not homotopy equivalent.
questions.explanation: $\pi_1(T^2) \cong \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$ is abelian, while $\pi_1(S^1 \vee S^1) \cong F_2$ is nonabelian. Non-isomorphic fundamental groups show the two spaces are not homotopy equivalent, hence not homeomorphic.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Recall the fundamental groups of the basic spaces.
questions.solution: The circle has $\pi_1(S^1) \cong$ {{1}}, generated by one loop. The number of generators in a minimal generating set for $\pi_1(S^1)$ is {{2}}. The sphere $S^2$ has trivial fundamental group, so it {{3}} simply connected.
questions.explanation: $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$ is infinite cyclic, generated by $1$ element. $S^2$ has trivial $\pi_1$ and is path-connected, so it is simply connected.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Compute the fundamental group of a product space.
questions.solution: The torus is the product $S^1 \times S^1$, so its fundamental group is the product of the factor groups: $\pi_1(T^2) \cong$ {{1}}. The number of independent commuting generators is {{2}}. Whether this group is abelian: answer {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\pi_1(T^2) \cong \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$, with $2$ commuting generators (meridian and longitude). The direct product of abelian groups is abelian, so yes.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Determine the fundamental group of real projective space.
questions.solution: The double cover $S^2 \to \mathbb{RP}^2$ has simply connected total space, so $\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^2)$ is the deck group, which is {{1}}. The order of $\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^2)$ is therefore {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The deck group of the double cover $S^2 \to \mathbb{RP}^2$ is $\mathbb{Z}/2$, which has order $2$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Count free generators and compare contractible spaces.
questions.solution: A wedge of three circles has fundamental group the free group on {{1}} generators. A convex subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ is contractible, so its fundamental group is the {{2}} group.
questions.explanation: A wedge of $n$ circles gives the free group $F_n$; for three circles that is $3$ generators. A convex set is contractible, so its fundamental group is trivial.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology
